items.title: Water Lilies (Agapanthus)
items.significance: Water Lilies (Agapanthus) turns a pond into a field of shifting color, light, and reflection—Monet painting the sensation of looking rather than a fixed view.

items.title: The Large Plane Trees (Road Menders at Saint-Rémy)
items.significance: The Large Plane Trees makes ordinary road work feel electrically alive; Van Gogh’s repeated marks give trunks, earth, and air the same restless energy.

items.title: The Burning of the Houses of Lords and Commons
items.significance: Turner’s Burning of the Houses of Lords and Commons records catastrophe as color and atmosphere, with fire dissolving architecture into light on the Thames.

items.title: The Thinker
items.significance: The Thinker gives concentration a physical weight: a body folded inward, every muscle carrying the strain of an idea that has not yet resolved.

items.title: The Age of Bronze
items.significance: The Age of Bronze rejected heroic poses for an uncertain waking gesture, helping sculpture feel like a living body caught between stillness and motion.

items.title: Indian Combat
items.significance: Indian Combat shows Edmonia Lewis shaping a tense, spiraling encounter in marble while claiming space in a nineteenth-century art world that rarely welcomed her.

items.title: Perumal Temple Vimana from Corner of Court, Madurai
items.significance: This 1858 view of Madurai’s Perumal Temple preserves architecture as accumulated sculpture, geometry, devotion, and the experience of moving through a court.

items.title: Interior of the Pantheon, Rome
items.significance: Panini’s Pantheon interior makes the Roman dome legible as an engineered atmosphere, using scale, light, and tiny visitors to reveal the room’s vastness.

items.title: Outer Staircase of a Gothic Ruin
items.significance: Outer Staircase of a Gothic Ruin treats a weathered building as a record of structure, repair, erosion, and the lives that continued around it.

items.title: Automobile
items.significance: This 1913 automobile shows the machine before its shape became visually ordinary: exposed wheels, carriage proportions, and mechanical parts still declaring a radical new way to move.

items.title: President Coolidge Gets a New Automobile
items.significance: President Coolidge’s new automobile captures the moment the car became both a practical machine and a public symbol, reshaping streets, status, work, and everyday movement.

items.title: Streamline Automobile
items.significance: Boysen Toys’ Streamline Automobile compresses early twentieth-century speed and industrial styling into a small wooden form built to be handled and imagined in motion.

items.title: Pocket Watch
items.significance: Robert Roskell’s pocket watch makes precision portable, enclosing gears, craft, status, and the social habit of measuring a day inside a hand-sized object.

items.title: Watch
items.significance: This Bautte et Moynier watch joins horology and jewelry, showing how a machine for keeping time could also be designed as an intimate object of display.

items.title: Pocket Watch
items.significance: Breguet et Fils turned the pocket watch into quiet engineering: a legible dial, disciplined proportion, and complex movement carried close to the body.

items.title: Chair
items.significance: This Herter Brothers chair turns sitting into a complete design problem, balancing ornament, joinery, material, posture, and the character of the room around it.

items.title: Prestige Chair
items.significance: The Prestige Chair shows furniture working as social language: its height, surface, and ceremony communicate authority before anyone takes a seat.

items.title: Tea Table
items.significance: Carlo Bugatti’s Tea Table makes furniture feel almost architectural, building an unusual silhouette from richly worked surfaces and sharply controlled geometry.

items.title: Woman’s Bridal Dress, Tabard
items.significance: This bridal tabard preserves fashion as ceremony, where cut, embroidery, symbolism, and the movement of a dressed body all participate in one public moment.

items.title: Dress
items.significance: This pre-Columbian dress keeps pattern and construction inseparable, showing clothing as a place where identity, labor, material, and daily life meet.

items.title: Woman’s Two-Piece Dress (Biil’éé)
items.significance: The Diné two-piece dress known as Biil’éé carries design through woven structure, proportion, and movement rather than treating decoration as something added later.

items.title: Still Life with Fish, Bread, and Kettle
items.significance: Meléndez gives fish and bread the seriousness of portrait subjects, turning a modest meal into a study of texture, nourishment, labor, and abundance.

items.title: Still Life with Herring, Bread, and Cheese
items.significance: Herring, bread, and cheese become a compact history of ordinary eating in Decamps’s still life, where humble ingredients receive precise, patient attention.

items.title: Still-Life with Crayfish, Oysters, and Fruit
items.significance: Cornelis de Heem’s feast of crayfish, oysters, and fruit makes food theatrical while preserving the fleeting ripeness, shine, and excess of a laid table.

items.title: Silver Wine Jug, Ham, and Fruit
items.significance: A silver wine jug anchors this still life, reflecting the room around it and showing how vessels, drink, hospitality, and display became one designed ritual.

items.title: Wine Cup with Children at Play
items.significance: This Ming wine cup turns drinking into a miniature narrative, wrapping children at play around porcelain meant to be lifted, turned, and viewed in the hand.

items.title: Gourd-Shaped Wine Pot
items.significance: The gourd-shaped wine pot borrows a form from nature and rebuilds it in ceramic, making pouring, holding, and visual wit part of the same act.

items.title: Sitar
items.significance: This nineteenth-century sitar joins resonant engineering with elaborate surface design, creating an instrument whose visual rhythm anticipates the music it can produce.

items.title: Figurative Harp (Domu or Kundi)
items.significance: The domu or kundi harp gives music a carved body, joining strings, resonance, and a human figure in an instrument meant to be heard and seen.

items.title: Koto (Zither)
items.significance: The koto’s long wooden body turns proportion into sound, with movable bridges allowing the instrument to be tuned for a particular performance.

items.title: First Bell Telephone
items.significance: The First Bell Telephone marks the startling idea that a voice could be translated into a signal, carried elsewhere, and reconstructed for another person to hear.

items.title: The Edison Concert Phonograph
items.significance: The Edison concert phonograph made recorded sound into a repeatable object, changing music, speech, memory, entertainment, and the meaning of a live moment.

items.title: First Typewriter
items.significance: The first typewriter reorganized writing around a keyboard and mechanism, accelerating office work while giving text a consistent mechanical form.

items.title: First Locomotive Built in America, Best Friend
items.significance: Best Friend makes the steam locomotive’s system visible: boiler, wheels, steel frame, and concentrated power designed to pull people and goods across distance.

items.title: Benjamin R. Stickney Standing Next to a Postage Stamp Printing Machine
items.significance: Stickney’s postage-stamp printing machine concentrates precision, repetition, and security into one production system built to reproduce tiny public objects at national scale.

items.title: Domestic Sewing Machine
items.significance: The domestic sewing machine concentrates repeated handwork into a foot-powered mechanism, changing the speed, scale, and conditions under which clothing could be made.

items.title: Carding Tool
items.significance: The carding tool embodies practical intelligence in its teeth and grip, preparing fibers so they can become thread, cloth, and the materials of daily life.

items.title: Combination Tool
items.significance: This combination tool joins multiple working edges in one hand-held object, turning material, leverage, and grip into a compact answer to several practical tasks.

items.title: Straw Splitting Tool
items.significance: The straw splitting tool converts a narrow material into consistent working strips, showing how a specialized hand tool can make delicate repeatable craft possible.

items.title: Animal Act with Baboon, Dog and Donkey
items.significance: This 1919 animal act survives from cinema’s early decades, when a short staged performance could turn motion itself into a new kind of recorded spectacle.

items.title: Roosevelt, Friend of the Birds
items.significance: Roosevelt, Friend of the Birds shows early nonfiction film building public memory by combining moving images, nature, personality, and civic storytelling.

items.title: Buster’s Dog to the Rescue
items.significance: Buster’s Dog to the Rescue captures early screen comedy experimenting with character, timing, action, and the simple magic of drawings or figures set into motion.

items.title: Attributes of Music
items.significance: Attributes of Music gathers instruments and notation into a quiet still life, treating musical possibility as something stored in crafted objects before a performance begins.

items.title: Annie Lawrie — Sheet Music Cover
items.significance: Winslow Homer’s Annie Lawrie cover shows how music circulated visually, with illustration helping a song travel through parlors, shops, and printed sheets.

items.title: Minnie Clyde — Sheet Music Cover
items.significance: The Minnie Clyde sheet-music cover belongs to music’s printed life, where typography, image, melody, commerce, and home performance shared a single page.

items.title: Mirror Case with a Couple Playing Chess
items.significance: This mirror case places a chess game inside an intimate object, linking strategy and courtship in a scene designed to be held close and opened.

items.title: A Gaming Board
items.significance: Peter Flötner’s gaming board turns rules into physical space, giving players a shared surface where chance, strategy, competition, and sociability can unfold.

items.title: The Game of Leap Frog
items.significance: The Game of Leap Frog preserves play as a designed social action: bodies, rhythm, rules, risk, and laughter coordinated without any manufactured equipment.

items.title: Gospel Book with Evangelist Portraits
items.significance: This Gospel book makes reading architectural, organizing portraits, initials, script, sequence, and binding into a durable object built for repeated attention.

items.title: Gospel Book with Commentaries
items.significance: The Gospel Book with Commentaries shows a book as layered conversation, placing primary text and interpretation together through careful copying and page design.

items.title: Books and Scholars’ Accoutrements
items.significance: Books and Scholars’ Accoutrements turns the tools of study into a visual world, presenting learning through shelves, bindings, objects, order, and cultivated attention.

items.title: Statue of Liberty, N.Y.
items.significance: The Statue of Liberty combines sculpture, structure, engineering, and political symbolism at harbor scale, designed to be encountered from a great distance.

items.title: Eiffel Tower and Park, Paris, France
items.significance: The Eiffel Tower turned exposed iron engineering into a public monument, using height, structure, and skyline placement to make modern construction itself the subject.

items.title: Lincoln Memorial
items.significance: Henry Bacon’s Lincoln Memorial uses a classical temple form to frame national memory, public gathering, speech, protest, and the long argument over citizenship.

items.title: Public Square, Cleveland, Ohio
items.significance: Cleveland’s Public Square shows a city making room for circulation, gathering, landscape, monuments, transit, and the unplanned encounters of public life.

items.title: La Alameda de México
items.significance: La Alameda de México presents the public park as shared civic design, where paths, trees, fountains, vendors, and crowds give a city space to breathe.

items.title: Public Casino, Belle Isle Park
items.significance: Belle Isle’s public casino shows recreation becoming civic infrastructure, with architecture and parkland designed for gathering beside the water.

items.title: Brooklyn Bridge and Brooklyn
items.significance: The Brooklyn Bridge joined stone towers, steel cables, calculation, and dangerous labor to make an unprecedented span part of everyday city movement.

items.title: Chicago Drainage Canal and Controlling Works, Lockport, Ill.
items.significance: Chicago’s drainage canal and controlling works reshaped a watershed through channels, gates, and coordinated labor, revealing both the ambition and lasting consequences of infrastructure at landscape scale.

items.title: Gatun Locks and Dam, Panama Canal
items.significance: Gatun Locks and Dam made ships climb across a continent by coordinating water, gates, excavation, power, and an immense international workforce.
